questions.text: What is pharmacology?

answers.text: The study of drugs to include their origin, properties, and interactions with living organisms
The study of how the body absorbs and eliminates chemicals
The study of how receptors respond to neurotransmitters
The study of drug manufacturing and distribution

questions.text: What are the 2 identifiers used to verify patient identity before administering medication?

answers.text: Insurance ID and date of admission

questions.text: What are the three phases of drug action?
What does 'administration' refer to in the phases of drug action?

answers.text: How the drug is metabolized by the liver
How the drug is made available to the body
How the drug is eliminated from the body
How the drug stimulates or blocks receptors

questions.text: Which route of administration is the route of preference for systemically acting drugs in emergency conditions?
Why does the inhalation route produce less side effects?

answers.text: It requires larger doses that are more targeted
It bypasses GI absorption and limits hepatic metabolism
It is only used for topical conditions

questions.text: What does NAVEL stand for?
When medications are delivered down the endotracheal tube, what dosage adjustment is required?

answers.text: Half the normal IV dose followed by 5mL saline flush
The same IV dose followed by 20mL saline flush
2–2.5 times the normal IV dose followed by 10mL saline flush
3 times the normal IV dose with no flush

questions.text: What does ADME stand for in pharmacokinetics?
What is pharmacokinetics?

answers.text: Mechanisms by which medications produce effects in the body
How the drug is made available to the body
Actions the body performs with the drug
How drugs act upon specific receptor sites

questions.text: What is pharmacodynamics?

answers.text: Actions the body performs with the drug
Mechanisms by which medications produce effects in the body
How the drug is absorbed and distributed
How the drug is eliminated from the body

questions.text: In the Lock and Key Theory, the neurotransmitter is the _____ and the receptor is the _____.
Adrenergic medications do what?

answers.text: Block receptors from responding to norepinephrine/epinephrine
Stimulate receptors to respond to acetylcholine
Block receptors from responding to acetylcholine

questions.text: Where are Alpha(1) receptors located?
When Alpha(1) receptors are stimulated, what do they cause?

answers.text: Vasoconstriction (increasing blood pressure) and an increased HR

questions.text: Alpha anti-adrenergic medications (the 'zosin' drugs) produce what effect?
Where are Beta 1 receptors located?
When Beta 2 receptors are stimulated, what occurs?

answers.text: Vasodilation (decreased BP) and bronchodilation (opening up the airways)

questions.text: Beta anti-adrenergic medications ('Beta Blockers') are the primary treatment for what condition?
Under the parasympathetic nervous system, the Cholinergic Pathway uses which type of receptors?
What happens when muscarinic receptors M1 and M3 are stimulated?
Anti-cholinergic medications block stimulation of M1 and M3, resulting in what?
According to the 'Tips and Tricks,' what is the key rule for receptor numbers?

answers.text: Odd numbers contract; even numbers relax

questions.text: Intraosseous (IO) administration involves injecting medications directly into what?
Agonistic medications do what to receptors?
